nativeRange.habitat: Deep outer reef walls, cave interiors, and overhangs in nutrient-rich coastal waters with moderate currents; typically collected from the outer continental shelf of southern Florida

decorPreferences: shaded areas away from direct light
attachment to rock via aquarium epoxy

diet.notes: The Orange Tree Gorgonian is fully non-photosynthetic — it produces no energy through light and survives entirely by capturing food particles from the water column. Suitable foods include phytoplankton, rotifers, copepods, brine shrimp nauplii, Mysis shrimp, cyclops, marine snow, oyster eggs, and commercial filter-feeder preparations. Three to four feedings per day are generally considered optimal; two daily feedings are commonly cited as a realistic minimum in experienced hands. Direct target feeding or broadcast feeding with reduced flow during feeding periods is recommended to improve food contact with the polyps.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS and SPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive corals placed in direct contact
cyanobacteria-prone systems (bacterial overgrowth rapidly degrades tissue)

summary: The Orange Tree Gorgonian (Swiftia exserta) is a vivid non-photosynthetic Caribbean sea fan that requires no light but depends entirely on multiple daily feedings of zooplankton. It is widely considered one of the more demanding corals in the reef hobby and is generally recommended for expert-level aquarists.
careNotes:
The Orange Tree Gorgonian is a fully non-photosynthetic gorgonian — it contains no symbiotic algae and therefore derives no energy from light, relying entirely on capturing planktonic food from the water column. It is generally considered expert-level due to its intensive feeding requirements and low tolerance for system instability.

Key care requirements:

Feeding: This is the single most critical factor. Feedings of 3–4 times daily are generally recommended; 2 feedings daily have been reported as a workable minimum in experienced hands. Suitable foods include phytoplankton, rotifers, copepods, brine shrimp nauplii, Mysis shrimp, Cyclop-Eeze, oyster eggs, and commercial filter-feeder preparations (e.g., Roti-Feast, TDO-A, Oyster Feast, R.O.E.). Reducing flow during feeding periods has been found to improve food contact. Without consistent feeding the colony tends to rapidly decline.

Lighting: Low intensity preferred to prevent nuisance algae and cyanobacteria from colonizing branches. Does not require light for survival. Placement in a shaded area, cave, or under a rock overhang is commonly recommended.

Flow: Moderate to strong flow is generally advised — the species is a filter feeder that relies on water movement to deliver food particles to its polyps. Flow should ideally be varied or pulsing rather than directly blasting the colony.

Placement: Can be placed anywhere in the tank as long as it receives adequate flow and low light. Colonies are typically secured to live rock using aquarium-safe epoxy. Trade specimens are generally 15–30 cm; wild colonies can reach 46+ cm in aquaria and up to 150 cm on natural reefs.

Cyanobacteria: Bacterial overgrowth on the branches causes rapid deterioration of polyps and tissue. This is a common cause of decline; maintaining good water flow and avoiding low-nutrient stagnant pockets around the colony tends to help.

Depth notes: Wild specimens are collected from deeper, dimly lit reef zones (typically 15–100 m off southern Florida and the Bahamas), with the species' full range extending to approximately 490 m. Trade specimens tend to come from the shallower end of this range. The species' deep-water origin suggests it may prefer cooler temperatures toward the lower end of the 21–25°C aquarium range, and it may have reduced thermal tolerance compared to shallow-reef corals.

Supplements: Strontium, iodine, calcium (generally advised 380–430 ppm), and trace elements are commonly recommended alongside regular water changes.
breedingNotes: The Orange Tree Gorgonian has been successfully spawned and reared through larval development in aquaria under research conditions, with results published in 2024. Sexual reproduction involves broadcast spawning; colonies are gonochoric (meaning male and female individuals are separate). Asexual fragmentation is also documented and may be feasible for propagation by experienced aquarists. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the general hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page; provided water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (expert), lighting (low), feeding notes, and supplements.

additional.name: TFH Magazine – Swiftia exserta
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (68–76°F / 20–24°C — cooler than LiveAquaria), pH (8.1–8.2), SG (1.025), feeding frequency (minimum 2x daily, 3–4x recommended), and native range (Florida/Bahamas, 50–100 ft depth). Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria to yield 21–25°C working range.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Orange Tree Gorgonian is a Rare, Deepwater Treat
additional.notes: Confirmed NPS status, deep-water origin (outer shelf, 50–1000+ ft), flow description (moderate upwelling currents), feeding protocol (3x daily: TDO-A, Roti-Feast, rotifers, copepods), and colony size/coloration. Growth noted as slow.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Orange Tree Gorgonian Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), confirmed strong flow, low lighting, peaceful temperament, Caribbean native range.

additional.name: ReefLex – Swiftia exserta
additional.notes: Confirmed aquarium size recommendation (500L), adult colony size (15–20 cm in trade), depth range (21–494 m), native range (West Atlantic: Canada, USA, Florida, Gulf of Mexico, Bahamas), feeding diet list, and daily feeding frequency. Wild temperature range (0.5–20.7°C) reflects deep/cold collection environment and was not used for aquarium husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Gulf Council Coral Portal – Orange Sea Fans of the Deep
additional.notes: Confirmed colony morphology (fan-shaped, orange to red, 6–18 in / 15–46 cm height), Western Atlantic distribution, moderate coastal current habitat, and NPS filter-feeder biology.
